fact: September 18 sits exactly 3 days before the autumn equinox, when day and night become perfectly equal twins!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when squirrels go absolutely bonkers collecting acorns - they can bury up to 10,000 nuts in a single season!
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets almost exactly due west around this date, making it super easy to find directions without a compass.
September 18 marks the peak time when monarch butterflies start their epic 3,000-mile journey to Mexico - that's like flying from New York to London!
This date falls smack in the middle of spider season - house spiders aren't actually coming inside, they've been there all along and are just getting big enough to notice!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, September 18 is prime baby animal season as spring kicks into high gear.
The moon looks extra huge and orange when it rises around this time of year because of all the dust and pollen floating in the autumn air.
September 18 is day 261 of the year, leaving exactly 104 days until New Year's Eve - that's almost perfectly three and a half months!
Trees in northern climates start pumping sugar down to their roots right around now, which is why maple syrup tastes so amazing in early fall.
This is peak migration madness for birds - over 5 billion birds are zooming across North America right now, mostly flying at night!
September 18 sits right in the sweet spot where both Virgo and Libra people celebrate birthdays, making it the most organized yet indecisive time of year.
